shorts_scripts.hook: Sprinting vs Jogging: See what happens inside your body.
beats: While jogging uses slow-twitch fibers and oxygen to burn steady fuel...
Sprinting flips a survival switch, instantly tearing open your fast-twitch muscle fibers.
This triggers 'EPOC'—causing your nervous system to melt fat at three times the speed even after you stop running.
shorts_scripts.payoff: Sprinting actually remodels your cellular mitochondria, turning you into a passive fat-burning machine even while asleep.
shorts_scripts.visual_note: 3D camera zooms into the thigh, showing slow-twitch fibers glowing blue during a jog, then violently exploding into pulsing red fast-twitch fibers during a sprint.

shorts_scripts.hook: What happens to your spine if you Deadlift every day?
beats: On days 1 to 5, your spinal erectors micro-tear, screaming for recovery.
By day 15, your central nervous system adapts, rapidly firing core-bracing signals.
By day 30, your body deposits calcium, drastically increasing the bone density of your vertebrae.
shorts_scripts.payoff: With perfect form, daily deadlifts pull fluid back into your spinal discs, making your back virtually bulletproof to aging.
shorts_scripts.visual_note: A translucent 3D skeleton deadlifting. Lumbar vertebrae glow red with mechanical stress, which gradually shifts to a thick, glowing green shield of protective muscle fibers.

shorts_scripts.hook: Planks VS Hanging Leg Raises: See inside your core.
beats: Planks lock your rectus abdominis in a static, isometric hold, building deep core endurance.
But Hanging Leg Raises force your abs to contract dynamically while fighting gravity.
This activates 64% more lower abdominal fibers and directly targets the hard-to-reach hip flexors.
shorts_scripts.payoff: Planks strengthen your inner corset, but leg raises are what actually build thick, deep V-line blocks.
shorts_scripts.visual_note: 3D torso showing static yellow tension lines across the entire midsection during a plank, switching to intense pulsing red waves climbing up the lower abs during a leg raise.
